text: Originally intended as an update to an article that had appeared on Bragdon’s Brewing Blog a couple of years ago this article delves more deeply into the historical development of the Balling formula, with particular regard to Balling’s calculation of alcohol factors and attenuation quotients as described in the texts he published in 1846 and 1854. An alternative version of the Balling formula is derived that can be used to calculate original extract from apparent extract and alcohol readings, obviating the need to employ Tabarie’s formula. The attenuation quotient (q) is also determined to be an important parameter, particularly for the craft brewers that are rapidly expanding their sector. The article also alludes to the ongoing value of the Balling formula, which, 155+ years after it was introduced, still allows a fermanted product’s original and post-fermentation extract to be calculated using a single measurement. An interesting and potentially significant ancillary observation is made regarding consumer information, which points to the value of providing a declaration of the real extract and alcohol content of the beverage, in order to allow consumers to get an accurate measure of the nutritional content of their drink.
explanation: Domain-specific detail and coherent historical discussion with natural variability in phrasing suggest human authorship; minor typographical irregularities are more typical of human writing than polished AI text.
